blocks.body: Modal Verbs help you talk about ability, possibility, obligation, advice, deduction, and polite interaction.

{"level":"A2-B2","intro":"In this section you will see how modal verbs work in real situations: when we talk about ability, possibility, obligation, advice, logical deduction, or polite requests.","rules":[{"label":"Ability / Possibility","color":"emerald","text":"Can, could, may, might help you talk about what is possible, real, or likely.","example":"She can drive. / It might rain later."},{"label":"Obligation / Advice","color":"blue","text":"Must, have to, should, ought to, need show obligation, necessity, and recommendation.","example":"You must wear a helmet. / You should rest."},{"label":"Deduction / Politeness","color":"amber","text":"Modal verbs also express deduction and different levels of politeness in requests and offers.","example":"He can't be serious. / Could you help me?"}]}

{"title":"1. What This Section Covers","intro":"The first stage of the Modal Verbs branch covers eight key topics:","rows":[{"en":"Can / Could","ua":"ability, possibility, permission, requests","note":"can swim / could help / Could you...?"},{"en":"May / Might","ua":"possibility, probability, formal permission","note":"may be late / might know / May I...?"},{"en":"Must / Have to","ua":"strong obligation and external necessity","note":"must leave / have to work"},{"en":"Should / Ought to","ua":"advice, recommendation, expectation","note":"should call / ought to check"},{"en":"Need / Needn't / Don't have to","ua":"necessity and absence of necessity","note":"need to study / needn't come"},{"en":"Modals of Deduction","ua":"present deduction based on evidence","note":"must be / might be / can't be"},{"en":"Past Modals","ua":"past deduction, regret, criticism","note":"must have gone / should have called"},{"en":"Requests / Offers / Suggestions","ua":"polite requests, offers, and ideas","note":"Could you...? / Shall I...? / Let's..."}],"warning":"⚠ Mustn't means prohibition, while don't have to means there is no necessity. Learners often mix these up."}

{"title":"2. What to Watch For","items":["After most modal verbs we use the base verb: can go, should study, might arrive.","Modal verbs do not take -s in the third person: She can swim, not She cans swim.","Past deduction often needs the pattern modal + have + V3: must have left, could have missed.","Negative forms do not all mean the same thing: mustn't = not allowed, don't have to = not necessary.","Politeness also changes with the modal: Could you...? is softer than Can you...?."]}
